Example 0 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 0
Source Text
Intervention for obese adolescents should be focused on developing healthy eating and exercise habits rather than on losing a specific amount of weight.
Generated Simplification
For teenagers who are overweight or obese, the main goal should be to build healthy habits around eating and physical activity. Instead of focusing on reaching a certain weight, it’s more important to work on long-term changes that support overall health.
Reference Simplification
The treatment of adolescent obesity is focused on developing healthy eating and exercise habits rather than on losing a specific amount of weight.
BERTScore
Precision
0.475
Recall
0.617
F1
0.546
Checklist Evaluation
Item Question Score Reason
L2 2 The simplification uses familiar language to explain the concept of healthy habits without introducing complex medical terms.
L3 N/A There are no abbreviations or acronyms in the source text.
L4 2 The simplification avoids medical jargon and uses simpler expressions like 'healthy habits' and 'long-term changes.'
C2 1 The simplification provides some background on the importance of healthy habits but could elaborate more on why these habits are beneficial.
C3 1 The simplification hints at the relationship between habits and overall health but does not explicitly explain the mechanism.
C4 0 No examples or comparisons are used to support understanding.
P1 2 The simplification clearly explains what the information means for teenagers who are overweight or obese.
P2 1 The simplification mentions focusing on habits rather than weight but does not fully explore possible options or consequences.
P3 0 The simplification does not include any warnings or next steps.
P4 0 The simplification does not suggest discussing any points with a healthcare professional.
Overall checklist comment
The simplification is clear and patient-friendly but could benefit from adding examples, warnings, and suggestions for professional consultation.
Medical Consistency Evaluation
Item Question Score Reason
M1 2 The main medical meaning of the source text is preserved in the simplification.
M2 2 The simplification does not add any unsupported medical information.
M3 2 The simplification does not omit any medically relevant information from the source text.
M4 2 The simplification preserves the focus on habits rather than specific weight loss, maintaining the original intent.
M5 2 The simplification does not make uncertain information sound certain.
M6 2 The simplification does not present itself as a replacement for professional medical advice.
Overall medical consistency comment
The simplification is medically accurate and preserves the intent and meaning of the source text without adding or omitting information.
Example 1 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 1
Source Text
The liver may be enlarged, hard, or tender; massive hepatomegaly with easily palpable nodules signifies advanced disease.
Generated Simplification
The liver may become larger than normal, feel firm or hard to the touch, or be painful when pressed. If the liver is very enlarged and has lumps that can be easily felt, this could be a sign of a more serious or advanced condition.
Reference Simplification
Typically, the liver is enlarged and hard. It may feel tender and often lumpy.

C2 1 Some background explanation is provided, but it could elaborate more on what 'advanced condition' means.
C3 2 The relationship between liver enlargement, nodules, and advanced disease is made explicit.

Example 2 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 2
Source Text
Frequency, urgency, and nocturia are due to incomplete emptying and rapid refilling of the bladder. Decreased size and force of the urinary stream cause hesitancy and intermittency. Pain and dysuria are usually not present. Sensations of incomplete emptying, terminal dribbling, overflow incontinence, or complete urinary retention may ensue.
Generated Simplification
You may experience symptoms like needing to urinate more often (frequency), feeling a strong urge to go (urgency), or waking up at night to urinate (nocturia). These happen because your bladder may not be fully emptying and is refilling quickly. Other possible symptoms include: - A weaker or slower urine stream, which can make it harder to start urinating (hesitancy) or cause your stream to stop and start (intermittency). - Feeling like your bladder isn’t completely empty after urinating. - Dribbling of urine at the end of urination (terminal dribbling). - Leaking urine unexpectedly (overflow incontinence). - In some cases, being unable to urinate at all (complete urinary retention). It’s important to note that pain or burning during urination (dysuria) is usually not part of this condition. If you’re experiencing any of these symptoms, it’s a good idea to talk to your healthcare provider. They can help figure out what’s causing the problem and discuss treatment options with you.
Reference Simplification
At first, men may have difficulty starting urination. Urination may also feel incomplete. Because the bladder does not empty completely, men have to urinate more frequently, often at night (nocturia).
BERTScore
Precision
-0.075
Recall
0.292
F1
0.103

Item Question Score Reason

L3 2 Abbreviations or acronyms are not present in the source text or simplification.
L4 2 Medical jargon is replaced with simpler expressions without changing the meaning.
C2 2 Short background explanations are provided for terms like 'frequency,' 'urgency,' and 'nocturia.'
C3 2 The simplification makes connections between symptoms and their causes explicit.
C4 1 No examples or comparisons are used, but they are not strictly necessary for understanding.
P1 2 The simplification clarifies what the symptoms mean for the patient’s situation.
P2 2 The simplification makes options and consequences explicit, such as discussing symptoms with a healthcare provider.
P3 2 Warnings and next steps, like consulting a healthcare provider, are clearly stated.
P4 2 The simplification indicates that the patient should discuss symptoms with a healthcare professional.
Overall checklist comment
The simplification effectively explains medical terms, clarifies patient implications, and provides clear next steps.
Medical Consistency Evaluation
Item Question Score Reason

Example 3 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 3
Source Text
Desmopressin
Generated Simplification
Desmopressin is a medication that is used to treat certain conditions related to the way your body handles water and urine. It works by mimicking a natural hormone in your body called vasopressin, which helps control how much water your kidneys remove from your blood. ### What is Desmopressin Used For? - **Diabetes Insipidus**: This is a condition where your body produces too much urine, leading to dehydration and excessive thirst. Desmopressin helps reduce the amount of urine your body makes. - **Bedwetting (Nocturnal Enuresis)**: In children or adults who have trouble staying dry at night, desmopressin can help by reducing urine production during sleep. - **Bleeding Disorders**: In some cases, desmopressin is used to treat mild bleeding problems, such as in people with von Willebrand disease or mild hemophilia A. It helps the blood clot more effectively. ### How is Desmopressin Taken? - It can come in different forms, such as tablets, nasal sprays, or injections. Your doctor will decide which form is best for you based on your condition. - Always follow your doctor’s instructions about how and when to take this medication. ### Important Things to Know - **Fluid Intake**: While taking desmopressin, your doctor may ask you to limit how much water or other fluids you drink. This is because drinking too much can lead to a condition called water retention, which can lower the sodium levels in your blood. Low sodium can cause symptoms like headaches, nausea, confusion, or, in severe cases, seizures. - **Side Effects**: Some people may experience side effects like headaches, stomach pain, or a stuffy nose (if using the nasal spray). Let your doctor know if you notice anything unusual or if side effects bother you. - **Monitoring**: Your doctor may want to check your blood sodium levels regularly, especially when you first start taking desmopressin or if your dose changes. ### What Should You Do? - Take the medication exactly as prescribed. - Follow any advice about limiting fluids. - Watch for symptoms like confusion, severe headaches, or nausea, and contact your doctor if they occur. - If you have any questions or concerns about desmopressin, talk to your healthcare provider. They can explain how it works and what to expect.
Reference Simplification
Sometimes, the drug desmopressin
BERTScore
Precision
-0.54
Recall
0.275
F1
-0.165
Checklist Evaluation
Item Question Score Reason
L2 2 Medical terms like 'diabetes insipidus' and 'vasopressin' are explained in simple language.
L3 2 No abbreviations or acronyms are present that require clarification.
L4 2 Unnecessary medical jargon is replaced with simpler expressions without changing the meaning.
C2 2 Background explanations for conditions like diabetes insipidus and bleeding disorders are provided.
C3 2 Connections between symptoms, risks, and treatments are made explicit, such as the link between fluid intake and sodium levels.
C4 1 Some examples are provided, but comparisons or additional examples could further enhance understanding.
P1 2 The text clarifies what the information means for the patient, such as how to take the medication and monitor for side effects.
P2 2 Options and consequences, such as limiting fluid intake and monitoring sodium levels, are made explicit.
P3 2 Warnings and next steps, like contacting a doctor for symptoms of low sodium, are clearly stated.
P4 2 The text advises discussing questions or concerns with a healthcare provider.
Overall checklist comment
The simplification is clear, patient-centered, and provides necessary explanations for a lay audience.
Medical Consistency Evaluation
Item Question Score Reason

M2 2 No unsupported medical information is added.

Example 4 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 4
Source Text
Some patients have weight loss, rarely enough to become underweight. Anemia, glossitis, angular stomatitis, and aphthous ulcers are usually seen in these patients.
Generated Simplification
Some people may lose weight, but it is rare for the weight loss to be severe enough to make them underweight. Other possible issues include: - **Anemia**: This means having fewer healthy red blood cells, which can cause tiredness, weakness, or pale skin. - **Glossitis**: This is when the tongue becomes swollen, red, and sometimes sore. - **Angular stomatitis**: Cracks or sores can form at the corners of the mouth, which may be painful. - **Aphthous ulcers**: These are small, painful sores that can appear inside the mouth. If you notice any of these symptoms, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help figure out the cause and recommend the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
Some people are undernourished, have mild weight loss and anemia, or have mouth sores and an inflamed tongue.

0.468

0.216

Example 5 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 5
Source Text
Involvement of the carotid and vertebral arteries results in reduced cerebral blood flow manifested by dizziness, syncope, orthostatic hypotension, headaches, transient visual disturbances, transient ischemic attacks, or strokes. Stenotic lesions in a subclavian artery near the origin of a patent vertebral artery can cause posterior circulation ischemic neurologic symptoms or syncope when the arm is used (called subclavian steal syndrome).
Generated Simplification
When certain arteries in the neck, called the carotid and vertebral arteries, are affected, it can lead to less blood reaching the brain. This reduced blood flow can cause symptoms such as: - Feeling dizzy. - Fainting (also called syncope). - A drop in blood pressure when standing up (orthostatic hypotension). - Headaches. - Temporary vision problems. - Brief episodes of stroke-like symptoms (transient ischemic attacks or TIAs). - Strokes. If there is a narrowing (called stenosis) in a large artery in the chest (the subclavian artery) near where it connects to a healthy vertebral artery, it can lead to a condition called subclavian steal syndrome. This happens because blood flow is redirected away from the brain when the arm on the affected side is used. Symptoms of this condition may include: - Neurological problems, such as dizziness or fainting, especially when using the arm. - Fainting episodes. If you experience any of these symptoms, it’s important to discuss them with your healthcare provider. They can help determine the cause and recommend the right treatment or further testing.
Reference Simplification
Head: People may feel dizzy or faint, have headaches, or have problems with vision.
BERTScore
Precision
0.083
Recall
0.373
F1
0.225

L3 2 Abbreviations like 'TIAs' are clarified as 'transient ischemic attacks.'
L4 2 Unnecessary medical jargon is replaced with simpler expressions, such as 'fainting' for 'syncope.'
C2 2 Background explanations are provided for terms like 'subclavian steal syndrome' and 'stenosis.'
C3 2 The relationships between symptoms, causes, and conditions are made explicit, such as how reduced blood flow leads to symptoms.
C4 1 No examples or comparisons are used, but the text is clear without them.
P1 2 The text clarifies what the symptoms and conditions mean for the patient.
P2 2 The text makes it clear that symptoms should be discussed with a healthcare provider and that further testing or treatment may be needed.
P3 2 Warnings about symptoms and the importance of consulting a healthcare provider are clearly stated.
P4 2 The text explicitly advises discussing symptoms with a healthcare provider.
Overall checklist comment
The simplification effectively explains medical terms, relationships, and patient-centered implications while maintaining clarity and accessibility.

Example 6 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 6
Source Text
However, if the neonate 's breathing appears obstructed, suctioning is done with an endotracheal tube attached to a meconium aspirator.
Generated Simplification
If a newborn baby seems to have trouble breathing because their airway is blocked, doctors may use a special procedure to help clear it. They insert a small tube, called an endotracheal tube, into the baby’s airway. This tube is connected to a device called a meconium aspirator, which helps remove anything causing the blockage.
Reference Simplification
If the newborn 's airway seems blocked by meconium, doctors try to suction it out. Doctors used to do suctioning whenever they saw meconium in the amniotic fluid or in the newborn 's mouth, but this has not been shown to help.

Example 7 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 7
Source Text
Photosensitivity occurs in some patients. Lupus erythematosus tumidus is characterized by pink to violaceous urticarial nonscarring plaques and/or nodules, some annular, in light -exposed areas.
Generated Simplification
Some people may experience photosensitivity, which means their skin reacts unusually to sunlight or other sources of light. A condition called lupus erythematosus tumidus can cause specific skin changes. These include: - Pink to purple patches or raised areas on the skin (these are called plaques or nodules). - These patches or nodules do not leave scars. - Some of these areas may have a ring-like shape (annular). - They usually appear on parts of the skin that are exposed to light, like the face, neck, or arms. If you notice these kinds of skin changes, especially after being in the sun, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help determine the cause and recommend the right treatment or precautions.
Reference Simplification
Rashes resulting from exposure to sunlight (photosensitivity) occur in some people with lupus, particularly fair-skinned people.

Example 9 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 9
Source Text
Recurrence rates after surgical excision are about 5 to 15 %.
Generated Simplification
When a growth or lesion is removed through surgery, there is a chance it could come back. This happens in about 5 to 15 out of every 100 cases. If you are concerned about recurrence, it’s important to discuss this with your doctor. They can explain your specific risks and whether any follow-up care or monitoring is needed.

After surgical removal, ganglia return in about 5 to 15 % of people.

Conjunctival redness, eye ache, foreign body sensation, photophobia, and lacrimation may be minimal initially.
Generated Simplification
At first, you might notice only mild symptoms in your eye. These can include: - Redness in the white part of your eye. - A dull ache or discomfort in your eye. - A feeling like something is stuck in your eye (even if nothing is there). - Sensitivity to light, which may make bright lights uncomfortable. - Increased tearing or watery eyes. If you experience these symptoms, it’s a good idea to monitor them closely. If they worsen or don’t improve, you should talk to a healthcare professional to find out what’s causing them and what treatment might be needed.

Corneal ulcers cause redness, pain, usually a feeling like a foreign object is in the eye (foreign body sensation), aching, sensitivity to bright light, and increased tear production.

Example 11 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 11
Source Text
In the most severe form (called malignant yellow fever), delirium, intractable hiccups, seizures, coma, and multiple organ failure may occur terminally.
Generated Simplification
In the most severe form of yellow fever, known as malignant yellow fever, serious symptoms can develop. These may include: - Confusion or difficulty thinking clearly (delirium) - Persistent hiccups that do not go away - Seizures (uncontrolled body movements) - Falling into a coma (a state of deep unconsciousness) - Failure of multiple organs in the body, which can be life-threatening If you or someone you know is experiencing symptoms of yellow fever, especially severe ones like these, it is important to seek medical care immediately.
Reference Simplification
Severe infection can cause seizures, malfunction of several organs, and coma may occur.

Precision
-0.018
Recall
0.414
F1
0.19

Example 12 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 12
Source Text
Patients with allergic conjunctivitis report having Bilateral mild to intense ocular itching; Conjunctival hyperemia; Photosensitivity (photophobia in severe cases); Eyelid edema; Watery or stringy discharge.
Generated Simplification
Allergic conjunctivitis is a condition that affects the eyes and is caused by an allergic reaction. People with this condition often experience the following symptoms: - **Itchy eyes**: Both eyes may feel mildly to intensely itchy. - **Redness in the eyes**: The white part of the eyes may appear red or bloodshot. - **Sensitivity to light**: Bright light may feel uncomfortable, and in more severe cases, it can cause pain. - **Swollen eyelids**: The eyelids may look puffy or swollen. - **Eye discharge**: You might notice a watery or sticky, stringy fluid coming from your eyes. If you have these symptoms, it’s a good idea to talk to your doctor. They can help confirm if it’s allergic conjunctivitis and recommend the best treatment for you.
Reference Simplification
People with all forms of allergic conjunctivitis develop intense itching and burning in both eyes.

Example 14 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 14
Source Text
In a few patients, the first manifestation of hepatocellular carcinoma is bloody ascites, shock, or peritonitis, caused by hemorrhage of the tumor.
Generated Simplification
In some patients, the first sign of liver cancer (called hepatocellular carcinoma) can be serious symptoms such as: - Bloody fluid buildup in the abdomen (known as bloody ascites). - A sudden drop in blood pressure, which can lead to shock. - Severe abdominal pain and swelling caused by inflammation in the lining of the abdomen (called peritonitis). These symptoms happen when the tumor bleeds. If you experience any of these symptoms, it is important to seek medical care immediately.
Reference Simplification
Occasionally, the first symptoms are sudden abdominal pain and shock (dangerously low blood pressure) caused by rupture or bleeding of the cancer.

Example 15 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 15
Source Text
The carbocation intermediate is formed in the reaction 's rate determining step is an sp2 hybridized carbon with trigonal planar molecular geometry.
Generated Simplification
In this chemical reaction, there is a step where a molecule temporarily forms a structure called a "carbocation." A carbocation is a carbon atom that has a positive charge because it has lost an electron. At this stage, the carbon atom is arranged in a flat, triangular shape, which scientists call "trigonal planar." This step is important because it controls how fast the entire reaction happens.
Reference Simplification
The carbocation intermediate formed in the reaction 's rate limiting step is an sp2 hybridized carbon with trigonal planar molecular geometry.

Example 16 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 16
Source Text
Physiological adaptations permit the organism to perform special functions such as making venom, secreting slime, and phototropism ), but also involve more general functions such as growth and development, temperature regulation, ionic balance and other aspects of homeostasis.
Generated Simplification
Your body has special ways of adapting to its environment to help it function properly. These adaptations allow it to do specific tasks, such as: - Producing substances like venom (in certain animals) or slime. - Responding to light, a process called phototropism (common in plants). Your body also uses adaptations for more general and essential functions, including: - Growing and developing over time. - Keeping your body temperature steady, even when the environment changes. - Maintaining the right balance of salts and other important chemicals in your body (this is part of a process called homeostasis, which helps keep everything in balance). These processes are all part of how your body stays healthy and works as it should. If you have questions about how your body regulates these functions, you can discuss them with your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
Physiological adaptations permit the organism to perform special functions ( for instance, making venom, secreting slime, phototropism ); but also more general functions such as growth and development, temperature regulation, ionic balance and other aspects of homeostasis.

Example 17 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 17
Source Text
Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP).
Generated Simplification
CPAP stands for Continuous Positive Airway Pressure. It is a treatment often used to help people who have sleep apnea, a condition where breathing stops and starts during sleep. Here’s how it works: - A CPAP machine gently pushes air into your airways through a mask you wear over your nose, mouth, or both while you sleep. - The steady flow of air helps keep your airways open, making it easier to breathe and reducing interruptions in your sleep. Why it’s important: - CPAP can improve your sleep quality, reduce daytime tiredness, and lower the risk of health problems linked to sleep apnea, such as high blood pressure or heart issues. If you think CPAP might help you or if you’ve been prescribed one, talk to your doctor about how to use it properly and what to expect. They can also help adjust the settings to make it as comfortable and effective as possible.
Reference Simplification
Measures to support breathing.

Example 20 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 20
Source Text
Hepatocellular carcinoma develops in 10 to 15 % of patients with alcoholic cirrhosis.
Generated Simplification
Liver cancer, specifically a type called hepatocellular carcinoma, can develop in about 10 to 15% of people who have alcoholic cirrhosis. Alcoholic cirrhosis is a condition where long-term heavy drinking damages the liver, causing scarring and making it harder for the liver to work properly. If you have alcoholic cirrhosis, it’s important to talk to your doctor about your risk of liver cancer. Regular check-ups and tests may help detect any problems early, when treatment is more effective.

Example 21 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 21
Source Text
With diaphragmatic involvement, splinting of the chest and pain in one or both shoulders may occur. Other manifestations of FMF include acute pleurisy (in 30 %); arthritis (in 25 %), usually involving the knee, ankle, and hip; an erysipelas - like rash of the lower leg; and scrotal swelling and pain caused by inflammation of the tunica vaginalis of the testis. Pericarditis occurs very rarely.
Generated Simplification
If the diaphragm (the muscle that helps you breathe) is affected, you might experience chest pain and pain in one or both shoulders. Other possible symptoms of Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) include: - Sudden inflammation of the lining around the lungs (pleurisy), which happens in about 30% of cases. This can cause sharp chest pain, especially when breathing deeply. - Joint pain and swelling (arthritis), which occurs in about 25% of cases. This usually affects larger joints like the knees, ankles, or hips. - A red, swollen rash on the lower leg that looks similar to a skin infection called erysipelas. - Swelling and pain in the scrotum, caused by inflammation around the testicles. A rare symptom is inflammation of the sac around the heart (pericarditis), which can cause chest pain. If you have any of these symptoms or concerns, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help determine if these symptoms are related to FMF or another condition and discuss treatment options with you.
Reference Simplification
Chest pain: About 30 % of affected people have chest pain. Chest pain, which is typically triggered by breathing, is caused by inflammation of the membranes surrounding the lungs (pleuritis) or, rarely, by inflammation of the sac surrounding the heart (pericarditis). Arthritis: About 25 % of people have inflammation of large joints (arthritis), such as the knees, ankles, and hips. Rash: A painful red rash that usually appears near the ankles may occur but is comparatively rare among affected people in the United States. Scrotum pain: In males, the thick-skinned sac that surrounds and protects the testes (scrotum) may swell and be painful due to inflammation of a testis (testicle).

0.352

0.225

Example 22 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 22
Source Text
In acquired lymphocytopenias, lymphocytopenia usually remits with removal of the underlying factor or successful treatment of the underlying disorder.
Generated Simplification
If you have acquired lymphocytopenia, which means a lower-than-normal number of lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell that helps fight infections), the condition often improves once the cause is addressed. This could happen by either: - Removing the factor causing the issue (such as stopping a medication or avoiding a harmful substance), or - Successfully treating the illness or condition that led to the problem. If you think this applies to you, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help identify the cause and recommend the right steps for treatment.
Reference Simplification
If the lymphocytopenia is the result of AIDS, combination therapy with at least three antiviral drugs of different classes can increase the number of T cells and lengthen survival. If an infection develops, a specific antibiotic, antifungal, antiviral, or antiparasitic drug directed against the infective organism is given.

Example 23 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 23
Source Text
The most significant long- term complication of FMF is Chronic renal failure caused by deposition of amyloid protein in the kidneys.
Generated Simplification
One of the most serious long-term problems of Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) is a condition called chronic kidney failure. This happens when a protein called amyloid builds up in the kidneys over time, damaging them and making it harder for them to work properly. If you have FMF, it’s important to work with your doctor to manage the condition and reduce the risk of this complication.
Reference Simplification
If not treated adequately, some people with familial Mediterranean fever develop amyloidosis. In amyloidosis, an unusually shaped protein called amyloid is deposited in the kidneys and in many organs and tissues, impairing their function. Amyloid deposits may damage the kidneys, eventually resulting in kidney failure.

Example 25 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 25
Source Text
Intubation and CPAP are indicated for respiratory distress, followed by mechanical ventilation and admission to the neonatal ICU as needed.
Generated Simplification
If a newborn is having serious trouble breathing (called respiratory distress), certain treatments may be needed to help them breathe. These treatments can include: - **Intubation**: This is when a small tube is placed into the baby’s airway to help them get enough oxygen. - **CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure)**: This is a machine that gently pushes air into the baby’s lungs through a mask or small tubes in the nose to make breathing easier. If these steps are not enough, the baby may need to be connected to a machine called a **mechanical ventilator**, which helps with breathing more fully. In such cases, the baby would also be cared for in a specialized hospital unit called the **neonatal intensive care unit (NICU)**, where they can receive close monitoring and advanced care. If your baby is experiencing breathing problems, the medical team will decide on the best treatment and explain the steps to you.
Reference Simplification
Newborns who have trouble breathing after delivery may need to have a breathing tube placed in their windpipe and be put on continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP). This technique allows newborns to breathe on their own while being given slightly pressurized oxygen. Newborns are admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) and are given supplemental oxygen and may be placed on a ventilator (a machine that helps air get in and out of the lungs) if necessary.

Recall
0.373

0.157

Acute bronchitis is a sudden inflammation of the airways in the lungs, often caused by an infection. If you or someone you know experiences similar symptoms, it’s important to seek medical advice promptly.

Example 28 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 28
Source Text
Predisposing factors include brain disorders (eg, dementia, stroke, Parkinson disease), advanced age, sensory impairment (eg, impaired vision or hearing), alcohol intoxication, and multiple coexisting disorders. Precipitating factors include use of drugs (particularly 3 new drugs), infection, dehydration, shock, hypoxia, anemia, immobility, undernutrition, use of bladder catheters (whether urinary retention is present or not), hospitalization, pain, sleep deprivation, and emotional stress.
Generated Simplification
Certain factors can increase the risk of developing certain health problems. These factors are divided into two groups: predisposing factors (things that make someone more likely to develop a problem over time) and precipitating factors (things that can trigger the problem more immediately). Here’s what you should know: ### Predisposing Factors These are long-term conditions or characteristics that make someone more likely to be affected: - **Brain disorders** like dementia, stroke, or Parkinson’s disease. - **Older age**, as the body becomes more vulnerable with time. - **Sensory problems**, such as difficulty seeing or hearing. - **Alcohol intoxication**, which can affect brain and body function. - **Having multiple health conditions at the same time**, which can strain the body. ### Precipitating Factors These are triggers that can bring on or worsen a problem more suddenly: - **Medications**, especially starting three or more new drugs at once. - **Infections**, which can stress the body. - **Dehydration**, when the body doesn’t have enough fluids. - **Shock**, a serious condition where blood flow is too low. - **Low oxygen levels** (hypoxia), which can affect the brain and body. - **Anemia**, a condition where the body doesn’t have enough healthy red blood cells. - **Being immobile**, such as staying in bed for long periods. - **Poor nutrition**, when the body isn’t getting enough nutrients. - **Bladder catheters**, even if there’s no problem with urine flow. - **Hospital stays**, which can be physically and emotionally stressful. - **Pain**, which can affect overall health. - **Lack of sleep**, which can make it harder for the body to recover. - **Emotional stress**, which can weaken the body’s ability to cope. ### What This Means for You If you or someone you care for has any of these risk factors, it’s important to be aware of them. Talk to your doctor about ways to reduce risks, manage existing conditions, and address any new symptoms or concerns.
Reference Simplification
Delirium can result from less severe conditions in older people and in people who have had a stroke or who have dementia, Parkinson disease, or another disorder that causes nerve degeneration. These conditions include Minor illnesses (such as a urinary tract infection); Severe constipation; Pain; Use of a bladder catheter (a thin tube used to drain urine from the bladder); Dehydration; Prolonged sleep deprivation; Sensory deprivation (including being socially isolated and not having access to needed eyeglasses or hearing aids).
BERTScore
Precision
-0.283
Recall
0.138
F1
-0.08

C3 2 Connections between risk factors and their effects are made explicit, such as how dehydration stresses the body.
C4 1 Some examples are used, but comparisons or additional examples could further enhance understanding.

Example 31 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 31
Source Text
Many males with breast cancer have inherited a BRCA mutation, but there are other causes, including alcohol abuse and exposure to certain hormones and ionizing radiation.
Generated Simplification
Some men who develop breast cancer have inherited a change in their genes called a BRCA mutation. This genetic change can increase the risk of certain cancers, including breast cancer. However, breast cancer in men can also happen for other reasons, such as: - Drinking too much alcohol over time (alcohol abuse). - Being exposed to certain hormones. - Being exposed to ionizing radiation, which is a type of radiation that can damage cells. If you are concerned about your risk for breast cancer or have a family history of cancer, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help assess your risk and discuss any steps you might need to take.
Reference Simplification
Many males with breast cancer have got it through a BRCA mutation, but there are other causes, including alcohol abuse and exposure to certain hormones and ionizing radiation.

A PET scan also commonly is done to assess the extent of disease as well as the cervical lymphatics.

A PET scan is a type of imaging test that helps doctors see how tissues and organs in your body are working. It is often used to check how far a disease has spread in the body. In this case, it can also help examine the lymph nodes in the neck, which are part of your immune system. If your doctor recommends a PET scan, they will explain why it is needed and what the results might mean for your care.

Example 34 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 34
Source Text
Other causes have been attributed to contaminants produced by outgassing of some types of building materials, volatile organic compounds ( VOC ), molds ( see mold health issues ), improper exhaust ventilation of ozone ( byproduct of some office machinery ), light industrial chemicals used within, or lack of adequate fresh-air intake/air filtration ( see Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value ).
Generated Simplification
Some health problems can be caused by substances or conditions in indoor environments. These include: - Chemicals released into the air from certain building materials or furniture. This process is called "outgassing." - Volatile organic compounds (VOCs), which are chemicals that easily turn into gases. They can come from paints, cleaning products, or adhesives. - Mold, which can grow in damp areas and may cause health issues like allergies or breathing problems. - Poor ventilation, such as when office machines release ozone (a type of gas) and the air isn’t properly filtered or refreshed. - Light industrial chemicals used indoors, which may also affect air quality. - Insufficient fresh air or ineffective air filters. Air filters are often rated using a system called Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value (MERV), which measures how well they trap particles. If you’re concerned about air quality in your home or workplace, consider discussing this with a healthcare professional or an expert in indoor air quality. They can help identify potential problems and suggest solutions.
Reference Simplification
Other causes have been contaminants produced by outgassing of some types of building materials, volatile organic compounds ( VOC ), molds ( see mold health issues ), improper exhaust ventilation of ozone ( byproduct of some office machinery ), using light industrial chemicals in the building, or lack of adequate fresh-air intake/air filtration ( see Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value ).

Precision
0.035
Recall
0.216
F1
0.125

Example 35 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 35
Source Text
Waist circumference and the presence of metabolic syndrome appear to predict risk of metabolic and cardiovascular complications better than BMI does.
Generated Simplification
Measuring your waist size and checking for a condition called metabolic syndrome can give a better idea of your risk for certain health problems than using BMI (Body Mass Index) alone. ### What this means: - **Waist circumference**: This is the measurement around your waist. A larger waist size can be a sign of higher health risks, especially for heart disease and diabetes. - **Metabolic syndrome**: This is a group of health issues that often occur together, such as high blood pressure, high blood sugar, unhealthy cholesterol levels, and extra fat around the waist. Having metabolic syndrome increases the risk of heart disease, stroke, and diabetes. ### Why this matters: - While BMI looks at your weight in relation to your height, it doesn’t show where fat is stored in your body. Fat around the waist is more closely linked to serious health problems. - Knowing your waist size and whether you have metabolic syndrome can help your doctor better understand your health risks and recommend steps to lower them. ### Next steps: - If you’re concerned about your waist size or think you might have metabolic syndrome, talk to your doctor. They can measure your waist, check for signs of metabolic syndrome, and guide you on how to reduce your risks.
Reference Simplification
Waist circumference is measured. This measurement helps identify and quantify abdominal (visceral) obesity, which is fat that accumulates around the waist and in the abdomen. Abdominal obesity is much more harmful than fat that is distributed throughout the body under the skin (subcutaneous fat).

Example 36 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 36
Source Text
Clinically evident chronic venous insufficiency may not cause any symptoms but always causes signs; postphlebitic syndrome always causes symptoms.
Generated Simplification
Chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) is a condition where the veins in your legs have trouble sending blood back to your heart. - CVI may not always cause noticeable symptoms, like pain or discomfort, but it does always lead to visible changes in the legs. These changes might include swelling, skin discoloration, or the appearance of varicose veins. - Postphlebitic syndrome, which can happen after a blood clot in the veins, always causes symptoms. These symptoms may include leg pain, swelling, or a feeling of heaviness. If you notice any of these signs or symptoms, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help figure out what’s causing the problem and discuss treatment options with you.
Reference Simplification
People with chronic venous insufficiency have leg swelling (edema), which typically is worse at the end of the day because blood must flow upward against gravity when a person is standing or sitting.

Example 38 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 38

Genital lesions cause painful epididymal swelling, deep perineal discomfort, or prostatic tenderness detected during rectal examination.

Painful sores or lesions in the genital area can lead to: - Swelling in the epididymis, a small tube at the back of the testicles that stores and carries sperm. - Discomfort in the perineal area, which is the region between the genitals and the anus. - Tenderness in the prostate gland, which may be noticed during a rectal exam performed by a healthcare provider. If you experience any of these symptoms, it’s important to talk to a doctor. They can help determine the cause and recommend appropriate treatment.
Reference Simplification
In men, the coiled tube on top of testes (epididymis) may swell, causing pain, or infection of the prostate gland (prostatitis) may cause discomfort.

Example 39 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 39
Source Text
Inflammatory breast cancer is characterized by peau d'orange, erythema, and enlargement of the breast, often without a mass. A nipple discharge is common.
Generated Simplification
Inflammatory breast cancer is a rare but serious type of breast cancer. It has some specific signs to watch for: - **Peau d'orange**: This means the skin of the breast may look dimpled or pitted, similar to the surface of an orange. - **Redness (erythema)**: The skin on the breast may appear red or inflamed. - **Swelling**: The breast may become larger or feel swollen. Unlike other types of breast cancer, this condition often does not cause a noticeable lump or mass in the breast. Another common symptom is **nipple discharge**, which means fluid may come out of the nipple without squeezing. If you notice any of these changes in your breast, it’s important to contact a healthcare professional as soon as possible. They can help determine the cause and discuss next steps for evaluation or treatment.
Reference Simplification
In inflammatory breast cancer, the breast is warm, red, and swollen, as if infected (but it is not). A discharge from the nipple is common. Often, no lump can be felt in the breast, but the entire breast is enlarged.

Example 40 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 40
Source Text
Acute ulcerative blepharitis is usually caused by bacterial infection (usually staphylococcal) of the eyelid margin at the origins of the eyelashes; the lash follicles and the meibomian glands are also involved. It may also be due to a virus (eg, herpes simplex, varicella zoster). Acute nonulcerative blepharitis is usually caused by an allergic reaction involving the same area (eg, atopic blepharodermatitis and seasonal allergic blepharoconjunctivitis, which cause intense itching, rubbing, and a rash; or contact sensitivity [ dermatoblepharoconjunctivitis ]).
Generated Simplification
Acute ulcerative blepharitis is a condition where the edges of your eyelids become inflamed, often due to a bacterial infection. This usually happens because bacteria, like *Staphylococcus*, infect the base of your eyelashes or nearby oil glands (called meibomian glands). In some cases, it can also be caused by a virus, such as the herpes simplex virus (which can cause cold sores) or the varicella-zoster virus (which causes chickenpox and shingles). There is another type of blepharitis called acute nonulcerative blepharitis. This type is usually caused by an allergic reaction in the same area of the eyelid. For example: - **Atopic blepharodermatitis** or **seasonal allergic blepharoconjunctivitis** can cause intense itching, redness, and a rash. These are often linked to allergies, like hay fever. - **Contact sensitivity** (also called dermatoblepharoconjunctivitis) happens when your eyelid reacts to something it touches, like makeup, skincare products, or other irritants. If you notice symptoms like redness, swelling, itching, or discomfort around your eyelids, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help figure out the cause and recommend the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
Disorders that may cause blepharitis include bacterial (typically staphylococcal) infection of the eyelids or the ducts of the deeper glands that open at the edges of the eyelids, certain viral infections (usually herpes simplex), and allergic reactions (to pollens or sometimes to eye drops).

Recall
0.52

Example 41 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 41
Source Text
Other manifestations of cirrhosis may be present. Cirrhosis, if compensated, may be asymptomatic. The liver is usually small; when the liver is enlarged, hepatic steatosis or hepatoma should be considered.
Generated Simplification
Cirrhosis is a condition where the liver becomes scarred and doesn’t work as well as it should. Here are some important points to understand: - **Cirrhosis can sometimes show no symptoms** if it is in an early stage and the liver is still managing to function well. This is called "compensated cirrhosis." - **The size of the liver can vary** in people with cirrhosis: - The liver is often smaller than normal because of the scarring. - If the liver is larger than normal, it could mean there is fat buildup in the liver (a condition called hepatic steatosis) or possibly a liver tumor (called hepatoma). If you have cirrhosis or are being evaluated for it, your doctor may check the size of your liver and look for any signs of these other conditions. It’s important to discuss any concerns or symptoms with your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
Symptoms may also result from the complications of cirrhosis (see Introduction, above). After cirrhosis develops, the liver usually shrinks.

Example 42 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 42
Source Text
Further evaluation may include enhanced CT or MRI of the head, neck, and chest; thyroid scan; barium swallow or bronchoscopy; and esophagoscopy.
Generated Simplification
Your doctor may recommend additional tests to better understand your condition. These tests could include: - **CT scan or MRI**: These are imaging tests that create detailed pictures of the head, neck, and chest to check for any abnormalities. - **Thyroid scan**: This test looks at how well your thyroid gland is working. The thyroid is a small gland in your neck that helps control your metabolism. - **Barium swallow**: For this test, you drink a liquid called barium, which helps highlight your throat and esophagus (the tube that connects your mouth to your stomach) on X-rays. - **Bronchoscopy**: This involves using a thin, flexible tube with a camera to look inside your airways and lungs. - **Esophagoscopy**: Similar to a bronchoscopy, this test uses a tube with a camera to examine the inside of your esophagus. Your healthcare provider will decide which tests are necessary based on your symptoms and medical history. If you have questions or concerns about these tests, be sure to discuss them with your doctor.
Reference Simplification
In addition to asking questions about a person 's medical history, doctors base the diagnosis on laryngoscopy. Laryngoscopy is examination of the larynx, with a thin, flexible viewing tube. Additional testing may include Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or computed tomography (CT) of the head, neck, and chest; A scan of the thyroid gland; X-rays of the esophagus (barium swallow).

Example 43 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 43
Source Text
Endotracheal intubation and continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) Mechanical ventilation as needed Supplemental oxygen as needed
Generated Simplification
If someone is having serious trouble breathing, doctors may use certain treatments to help them get enough oxygen. These treatments include: - **Endotracheal intubation**: This is when a tube is placed through the mouth and into the windpipe to help with breathing. A machine may then assist in moving air in and out of the lungs. - **Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP)**: This involves wearing a mask that gently pushes air into the lungs to keep the airways open and make breathing easier. - **Supplemental oxygen**: If needed, extra oxygen can be provided through a mask or small tubes placed in the nose to ensure the body gets enough oxygen. These treatments are used depending on how severe the breathing problem is. Your healthcare team will decide which option is best for your situation and will explain the process to you. If you have questions or concerns, talk to your doctor or nurse.
Reference Simplification
Sometimes suctioning of the airways Measures to support breathing

Example 44 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 44
Source Text
In adults, BMI, defined as weight (kg) divided by the square of the height (m2), is used to screen for overweight or obesity (see table Body Mass Index (BMI)): Overweight = 25 to 29.9 kg/m2; Obesity = 30 kg/m2.
Generated Simplification
BMI, or Body Mass Index, is a way to check if a person’s weight is in a healthy range based on their height. It is calculated by dividing a person’s weight in kilograms (kg) by the square of their height in meters (m²). Here’s what the numbers mean for adults: - A BMI between 25 and 29.9 is considered "overweight." - A BMI of 30 or higher is classified as "obesity." BMI is often used as a first step to see if someone might have weight-related health risks. If you’re concerned about your BMI or what it means for your health, it’s a good idea to talk with your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Obesity is diagnosed by determining the BMI.

Example 45 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 45
Source Text
History of present illness should include the following: Whether the current discharge is unilateral or bilateral; What its color is; How long it has lasted; Whether it is spontaneous or occurs only with nipple stimulation; Whether a mass or breast pain is present.
Generated Simplification
When discussing your current symptoms with your doctor, it’s important to share specific details about any nipple discharge you may be experiencing. Here are the key points to mention: - Is the discharge coming from one breast (unilateral) or both breasts (bilateral)? - What color is the discharge? For example, is it clear, milky, yellow, green, or bloody? - How long has the discharge been happening? Has it been recent or ongoing for a while? - Does the discharge happen on its own (spontaneous), or does it only occur when the nipple is touched or squeezed? - Do you have any other symptoms, such as a lump in the breast or breast pain? Providing this information will help your doctor better understand your situation and decide if further tests or treatments are needed. If you’re unsure about any of these details, try to observe your symptoms closely before your appointment.
Reference Simplification
Nipple discharge is a cause for concern when it Occurs without the nipple 's being squeezed or stimulated by other means (when it occurs spontaneously); Occurs in women aged 40 or older; Comes from only one breast; Is bloody or pink; Is accompanied by a lump that can be felt; Occurs in a boy or man.

Example 46 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 46
Source Text
Patients with positive fecal occult blood tests require colonoscopy, as do those with lesions seen during sigmoidoscopy or an imaging study.
Generated Simplification
If your test for hidden blood in your stool (called a fecal occult blood test) comes back positive, you will need a colonoscopy. A colonoscopy is a procedure where a doctor uses a thin, flexible tube with a camera to look inside your colon (large intestine) for any problems. You will also need a colonoscopy if any unusual growths or abnormalities (called lesions) are found during other tests, such as a sigmoidoscopy (a test that looks at the lower part of your colon) or an imaging study (like a CT scan). If this applies to you, talk to your doctor about scheduling a colonoscopy. This test is important for checking your colon health and identifying any issues early.
Reference Simplification
People with blood in their stool require colonoscopy, as do those with abnormalities seen during a sigmoidoscopy or an imaging study.

Example 47 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 47
Source Text
A feeling of scratchiness or of a foreign body may be caused by either a conjunctival or a corneal disorder.
Generated Simplification
You might feel like there’s something stuck in your eye or experience a scratchy sensation. This can happen because of a problem with either the conjunctiva (the thin, clear tissue covering the white part of your eye and the inside of your eyelids) or the cornea (the clear, dome-shaped surface that covers the front of your eye). If you notice these symptoms, it’s a good idea to talk to an eye doctor to find out what’s causing the discomfort and how it can be treated.
Reference Simplification
A feeling of scratchiness or a foreign body sensation may be caused by a disorder of the conjunctiva (the thin membrane that lines the eyelid and covers the front of the eye) or of the cornea.

Example 49 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 49
Source Text
Vasculitic skin lesions may include mottled erythema on the palms and fingers, periungual erythema, nail -fold infarcts, urticaria, and palpable purpura. Petechiae may develop secondary to thrombocytopenia.
Generated Simplification
Skin problems caused by inflammation in the blood vessels (called vasculitis) can show up in different ways. Here are some examples of what you might notice on your skin: - **Mottled redness** on the palms of your hands or fingers. - **Redness around the nails** (periungual erythema). - **Small areas of damaged skin near the nails** (nail-fold infarcts). - **Hives** (raised, itchy patches on the skin). - **Raised purple or red spots** (palpable purpura) that you can feel when you touch them. In some cases, you might also see **tiny red or purple dots on the skin** (petechiae). These can happen if your blood has a lower-than-normal number of platelets, which are cells that help with clotting. This condition is called thrombocytopenia. If you notice any of these skin changes, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help figure out what’s causing the problem and recommend the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
Mottled red areas on the sides of the palms and up the fingers, redness and swelling around the nails, and flat, reddish purple blotches between the knuckles on the inner surfaces of the fingers also may occur. Purplish spots (petechiae) may occur because of bleeding in the skin as a result of low platelet levels in the blood.

Example 50 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 50
Source Text
Disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI), also called the arthritis - dermatitis syndrome, reflects bacteremia and typically manifests with fever, migratory pain or joint swelling (polyarthritis), and pustular skin lesions. In some patients, pain develops and tendons (eg, at the wrist or ankle) redden or swell. Skin lesions occur typically on the arms or legs, have a red base, and are small, slightly painful, and often pustular. DGI can mimic other disorders that cause fever, skin lesions, and polyarthritis (eg, the prodrome of hepatitis B infection or meningococcemia); some of these other disorders (eg, reactive arthritis) also cause genital symptoms.
Generated Simplification
Disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI) is a condition caused by the spread of a bacterial infection through the bloodstream. It is sometimes called "arthritis-dermatitis syndrome" because it often affects the joints and skin. Here’s what you need to know: ### Symptoms of DGI: - **Fever**: You may feel unusually warm or have chills. - **Joint pain or swelling**: Pain can move from one joint to another (like your knees, wrists, or ankles), and the joints may feel swollen or tender. - **Skin changes**: Small, red, and slightly painful spots may appear on your arms or legs. These spots often have pus inside (pustules). - **Tendon pain or swelling**: You might feel pain or notice redness and swelling in areas where tendons connect muscles to bones, such as your wrists or ankles. ### How DGI Can Be Confused with Other Conditions: DGI can look similar to other illnesses that cause fever, joint pain, and skin problems, such as: - **Hepatitis B infection**: A viral infection that can cause flu-like symptoms and joint pain before affecting the liver. - **Meningococcemia**: A serious bacterial infection that can cause fever, rash, and other symptoms. - **Reactive arthritis**: A condition that can cause joint pain and sometimes genital symptoms after an infection. ### What This Means for You: - If you have symptoms like fever, joint pain, or unusual skin spots, it’s important to see a healthcare provider. These symptoms could be caused by DGI or another condition that needs medical attention. - DGI is treatable, but early diagnosis is important to prevent complications. - Be sure to mention any recent sexual activity or other symptoms (like genital discomfort) to your doctor, as this can help them identify the cause of your symptoms. ### Next Steps: - Contact a healthcare professional if you notice any of these symptoms. - Follow your doctor’s advice for testing and treatment, which may include antibiotics to clear the infection. If you’re unsure about anything or have concerns, don’t hesitate to ask your doctor for more information.
Reference Simplification
Rarely, disseminated gonococcal infection (arthritis - dermatitis syndrome) develops. It occurs when the infection spreads through the bloodstream to other parts of the body, especially the skin and joints. Joints become swollen, tender, and extremely painful, limiting movement. The skin over infected joints may be red and warm. People typically have a fever, feel generally ill, and develop arthritis in one or more joints. Small, red spots may appear on the skin, usually on the arms and legs. The spots are slightly painful and may be filled with pus.
BERTScore
Precision
-0.054
Recall
0.273
F1
0.106

Example 52 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 52
Source Text
Dyspnea, tachypnea, and inspiratory stridor may be present, often causing the child to sit upright, lean forward, and hyperextend the neck with the jaw thrust forward and mouth open in an effort to enhance air exchange (tripod position). Relinquishing this position may herald respiratory failure. Suprasternal, supraclavicular, and subcostal inspiratory retractions may be present.
Generated Simplification
Some children may have trouble breathing, which can show up as: - **Dyspnea**: Feeling short of breath. - **Tachypnea**: Breathing faster than normal. - **Inspiratory stridor**: A high-pitched, noisy sound when breathing in. When this happens, the child might sit in a specific way to make breathing easier. This position is called the "tripod position." In this position, the child: - Sits upright. - Leans forward. - Tilts their head back, with their neck stretched and jaw pushed forward. - Keeps their mouth open. If the child stops sitting in this position, it could be a sign that their breathing is getting worse and they may be at risk of respiratory failure (when the lungs can’t get enough oxygen into the body). You might also notice the skin around certain areas of the chest pulling in with each breath. This can happen: - Above the breastbone (suprasternal retractions). - Above the collarbones (supraclavicular retractions). - Below the ribs (subcostal retractions). If you see these signs, it’s important to seek medical help right away.
Reference Simplification
As swelling of the epiglottis starts to narrow the airway, the child first begins to make a squeaking noise when breathing in (stridor) and then has progressively worse trouble breathing. The condition progresses rapidly.

Precision
-0.084
Recall
0.315
F1
0.109

Source Text
Respiratory stimulants (eg, caffeine)
Generated Simplification
Medications called respiratory stimulants, such as caffeine, are sometimes used to help improve breathing. These medicines work by stimulating the part of the brain that controls breathing. If you have a condition that affects your ability to breathe properly, your doctor might consider using a respiratory stimulant as part of your treatment plan. If you have questions about whether this type of medication is right for you, it’s important to discuss it with your healthcare provider. They can explain the potential benefits, risks, and whether it fits your specific needs.

Example 54 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 54
Source Text
Lewis acids such as boron trifluoride, zinc chloride, iron ( III ) chloride, and aluminium chloride are also useful catalysts.
Generated Simplification
Certain substances, called Lewis acids, can help speed up chemical reactions. Examples of these substances include boron trifluoride, zinc chloride, iron (III) chloride, and aluminum chloride. These are often used as catalysts, which means they help the reaction happen without being used up themselves. This information is more relevant in a laboratory or industrial setting and is not typically something patients need to worry about in their daily lives.
Reference Simplification
Lewis acids such as boron trifluoride, zinc chloride, iron chloride, and aluminium chloride are also useful catalysts.

Example 55 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 55
Source Text
However, a positive test for blood can result from nonmalignant disorders (eg, ulcers, diverticulosis), and a negative test does not rule out cancer because cancers do not bleed continuously.
Generated Simplification
A positive test result for blood in your stool does not always mean cancer. It can also happen due to other, less serious conditions like ulcers (sores in the stomach or intestines) or diverticulosis (small bulges in the wall of the intestine). On the other hand, a negative test result does not completely rule out cancer. This is because some cancers may not bleed all the time, and the test might miss them. If you have concerns about your test results, it’s important to discuss them with your doctor. They can help you understand what the results mean and whether further tests are needed.
Reference Simplification
However, many disorders besides cancer can cause blood in the stool and not all cancers bleed at all times.

Example 57 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 57
Source Text
For patients with type 1 and some variants of type 2 VWD, desmopressin, an analog of vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone) that stimulates release of VWF into the plasma and may increase levels of factor VIII, can be helpful. Desmopressin is ineffective in other variants of type 2 and in type 3 VWD, and in type 2A can exacerbate thrombocytopenia. To ensure adequate response to the drug, physicians give patients a test dose and measure the response of VWF antigen. Desmopressin 0.3 mcg/kg given in 50 mL of 0.9 % saline solution IV over 15 to 30 minutes may enable patients to undergo minor procedures (eg, tooth extraction, minor surgery) without needing replacement therapy. For many patients, intra- nasal desmopressin may be as effective as IV treatment and is often useful to prevent bleeding during minor surgical procedures.
Generated Simplification
Desmopressin is a medication that can help some patients with certain types of von Willebrand disease (VWD), a condition that affects blood clotting. Here’s what you need to know: ### How Desmopressin Works - Desmopressin is a medicine that helps release von Willebrand factor (VWF) into the blood. VWF is a protein that helps your blood clot properly. - It can also increase levels of another clotting protein called factor VIII. ### Who Can Use Desmopressin? - It is often helpful for people with **type 1 VWD** and some forms of **type 2 VWD**. - However, it does **not work** for people with **type 3 VWD** or certain other forms of type 2 VWD. In one type (called type 2A), it can even make a condition called thrombocytopenia (low platelet levels) worse. ### Testing Before Use - To make sure desmopressin will work for you, your doctor will give you a small test dose and check how your body responds. This involves measuring your VWF levels after the test dose. ### How Desmopressin is Used - For minor procedures like tooth extractions or small surgeries, desmopressin may help control bleeding so you don’t need additional treatments. - It is usually given in one of two ways: 1. **Through an IV (intravenous line):** A dose of 0.3 micrograms per kilogram of body weight is mixed with saline (a saltwater solution) and given slowly over 15 to 30 minutes. 2. **As a nasal spray:** For many patients, the nasal spray works just as well as the IV form and is convenient for preventing bleeding during minor procedures. ### What This Means for You - If you have VWD, your doctor will decide if desmopressin is a good option for you based on your specific type of VWD. - If desmopressin is suitable, it may help you avoid more complex treatments during minor surgeries or dental work. - Always discuss your treatment options with your doctor, especially if you are planning a procedure or have concerns about bleeding. If you have any questions about desmopressin or your VWD type, talk to your healthcare provider. They can guide you on the best approach for managing your condition.
Reference Simplification
For some mild forms of von Willebrand disease, drug treatment with desmopressin may be given to increase the amount of von Willebrand factor long enough for surgery or dental procedures to be done without transfusions.

Example 58 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 58
Source Text
Fungi and mycobacteria can cause hematogenous osteomyelitis, usually in immunocompromised patients or in areas of endemic infection with histoplasmosis, blastomycosis, or coccidioidomycosis.
Generated Simplification
Certain types of infections caused by fungi or a group of bacteria called mycobacteria can lead to a condition called hematogenous osteomyelitis. This is a serious infection that affects the bones and spreads through the bloodstream. - **Who is at risk?** This condition is more likely to occur in people with weakened immune systems (for example, due to certain illnesses or medications). It can also happen in areas where specific fungal infections, such as histoplasmosis, blastomycosis, or coccidioidomycosis, are more common. - **What does this mean for you?** If you have a weakened immune system or live in or have traveled to areas where these fungal infections are common, it’s important to be aware of symptoms like bone pain, fever, or swelling. These could be signs of a bone infection. - **What should you do?** If you think you might be at risk or are experiencing symptoms, talk to your doctor. They can help determine if further testing or treatment is needed.
Reference Simplification
Bacteria or fungal seeds (called spores) may infect the bone directly through open fractures, during bone surgery, or from contaminated objects that pierce the bone. Osteomyelitis may occur where a piece of metal has been surgically attached to a bone, as is done to repair a hip or other fracture (see Figure: Repairing a Fractured Hip). Also, bacteria or fungal spores may infect the bone to which an artificial joint (prosthesis) is attached (see Artificial Joint Infectious Arthritis). The organisms may be carried into the area of bone surrounding the artificial joint during the operation to replace the joint, or the infection may occur later.

These checks help identify if there might be a problem that needs further attention. If you have concerns about your breathing or feel feverish, let your healthcare provider know.

Example 63 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 63
Source Text
The severity of the diarrhea and vomiting can lead to rapid dehydration and electrolyte imbalance, and death in some cases.
Generated Simplification
Severe diarrhea and vomiting can cause your body to lose a lot of water and important minerals (called electrolytes) very quickly. This can lead to dehydration, which means your body doesn’t have enough fluids to work properly. In some cases, this can become life-threatening if not treated. If you or someone else has severe diarrhea and vomiting, it’s important to watch for signs of dehydration, such as feeling very thirsty, having a dry mouth, feeling weak or dizzy, or not urinating as much as usual. Contact a healthcare professional right away if you notice these symptoms.
Reference Simplification
The diarrhea and vomiting, in turn, can cause very bad dehydration, leading to death if untreated.

Example 64 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 64
Source Text
With chronic pain (> 2 to 3 weeks), the most common causes are TMJ dysfunction; Chronic eustachian tube dysfunction; Chronic otitis externa. Also with chronic pain, a tumor must be considered, particularly in older patients and if the pain is associated with ear drainage.
Generated Simplification
If you’ve been experiencing ear pain for more than 2 to 3 weeks, it’s important to know what might be causing it. Here are some common reasons for long-lasting ear pain: - **TMJ dysfunction**: This refers to problems with the joint that connects your jaw to your skull (the temporomandibular joint). Issues with this joint can sometimes cause pain that feels like it’s coming from your ear. - **Chronic eustachian tube dysfunction**: The eustachian tube helps drain fluid from your middle ear and keeps pressure balanced. If it doesn’t work properly over time, it can lead to discomfort or pain. - **Chronic otitis externa**: This is a long-term infection or irritation of the outer ear canal, sometimes called "swimmer’s ear." In some cases, especially in older adults or if the ear pain is accompanied by ear drainage (fluid or discharge coming from the ear), a tumor could be a possible cause. While this is less common, it’s something that should be checked by a healthcare professional. ### What should you do? - If your ear pain has lasted more than 2 to 3 weeks, it’s important to see a doctor. They can help figure out the cause and recommend the right treatment. - Be sure to mention any additional symptoms, like ear drainage, to your doctor, as this can help them make a diagnosis. Don’t ignore ongoing ear pain—it’s always better to get it checked out.
Reference Simplification
With chronic pain (pain for more than 2 to 3 weeks), the most common causes are TMJ disorders; Chronic eustachian tube dysfunction; Chronic external ear infection. A less common cause of chronic pain is pain from disorders affecting the larynx (voice box), including cancer (called referred pain).

Example 66 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 66
Source Text
About 5 to 10 % of people with variola major develop either a hemorrhagic or a malignant (flat) variant. The hemorrhagic form is rarer and has a shorter, more intense prodrome, followed by generalized erythema and cutaneous and mucosal hemorrhage.
Generated Simplification
Around 5 to 10% of people with variola major, a severe form of smallpox, may develop one of two rare and serious types: the hemorrhagic form or the malignant (flat) form. - **Hemorrhagic form**: This is less common and progresses quickly. It starts with a short period of intense early symptoms (called a prodrome), such as fever and feeling very unwell. After this, people may develop widespread redness of the skin (erythema) and bleeding in the skin and mucous membranes (the moist linings inside areas like the mouth and nose). If you have concerns about smallpox or its symptoms, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional for guidance.
Reference Simplification
In a few people with the severe form, the initial symptoms develop more quickly and are more intense. One rare type, called hemorrhagic smallpox causes bleeding. After a few days, bleeding occurs in the skin, mucous membranes, and gastrointestinal tract.

Sucking out the pus with a needle is often not sufficient.Skin abscesses are common and have become more common in recent years .

Draining pus with a needle alone is often not enough to treat a skin abscess. Skin abscesses are pockets of pus that form under the skin, usually due to an infection. They are fairly common and have been occurring more frequently in recent years. If you think you have a skin abscess, it’s important to see a healthcare professional. They can determine the best treatment, which may involve draining the abscess and possibly other steps to fully address the infection.

Modern medicine, both conventional and alternative, recognises that the different rheumatic disorders have different causes ( and several of them have multiple causes ) and require different kinds of treatment.

Different types of rheumatic disorders, which are conditions that affect your joints, muscles, or bones, can have different causes. Some of these conditions may even have more than one cause. It’s important to work with your healthcare provider to understand the cause of your condition and find the right treatment for you.

Example 71 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 71
Source Text
The reaction produces the corresponding sultine through a hetero-Diels-Alder reaction under kinetic control ( 60 °C ), but, under thermodynamic control ( 40 °C ), the reaction produces the corresponding sulfolene through a cheletropic reaction.
Generated Simplification
When this chemical reaction happens, the outcome depends on the temperature: - At a higher temperature (60°C), the reaction creates a compound called "sultine" through a specific type of chemical process. - At a lower temperature (40°C), the reaction produces a different compound called "sulfolene" through another type of chemical process. This explanation is about how temperature affects the result of the reaction. If this relates to your treatment or condition, your healthcare provider can explain how it applies to you.
Reference Simplification
The reaction makes the corresponding sultine through a hetero-Diels-Alder reaction under kinetic control (60 °C ), but, under thermodynamic control ( 40 °C ), the reaction makes the corresponding sulfolene through a cheletropic reaction.

Example 74 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 74
Source Text
Animal hoarding is a form of hoarding disorder in which patients accumulate a large number of animals and do not provide adequate nutrition, sanitation, and veterinary care despite deterioration of the animals (eg, weight loss, illness) and/or environment (eg, extreme overcrowding, highly unsanitary conditions).
Generated Simplification
Animal hoarding is a type of hoarding disorder. People with this condition keep a large number of animals but are unable to properly care for them. This often means the animals do not get enough food, live in dirty conditions, or do not receive necessary medical care. Over time, this can lead to serious problems, such as: - Animals becoming sick or losing weight. - Living spaces becoming extremely crowded or unsanitary. If you or someone you know is struggling with this issue, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional for guidance and support.
Reference Simplification
In animal hoarding, people accumulate more animals as pets than they have room for and can feed and provide veterinary care for. They allow the animals to live in unsanitary conditions. Often, the animals are overcrowded and lose weight and/or become ill. However, many people with the disorder do not recognize that they are not taking adequate care of the animals.

0.386

0.274

Example 79 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 79
Source Text
In 2010 at The Meeting of the States Parties to the Convention on the Prohibition of the Development, Production and Stockpiling of Bacteriological ( Biological ) and Toxin Weapons and Their Destruction in Geneva the sanitary epidemiological reconnaissance was suggested as well-tested means for enhancing the monitoring of infections and parasitic agents, for practical implementation of the International Health Regulations ( 2005 ).
Generated Simplification
In 2010, during a meeting in Geneva about international rules to prevent the use of harmful biological and toxin weapons, experts discussed a method called "sanitary epidemiological reconnaissance." This method is a proven way to monitor and track infections and diseases caused by parasites. It was suggested as a practical tool to help countries follow the International Health Regulations (IHR), which are global guidelines created in 2005 to improve health and safety worldwide.
Reference Simplification
In 2010 at The Meeting of the States Parties to the Convention on the Prohibition of the Development, Production and Stockpiling of Bacteriological ( Biological ) and Toxin Weapons and Their Destruction in Geneva the sanitary epidemiological reconnaissance was suggested as means for monitoring of infections and parasitic agents and for practical implementation of the International Health Regulations ( 2005 ).

Example 80 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 80
Source Text
As in trigeminal neuralgia, paroxysmal attacks of unilateral brief, excruciating pain occur spontaneously or are precipitated when areas innervated by the glossopharyngeal nerve are stimulated (eg, by chewing, swallowing, coughing, talking, yawning, or sneezing).
Generated Simplification
Glossopharyngeal neuralgia is a condition that causes sudden, intense pain on one side of the throat, tongue, or ear. This pain can happen without warning or be triggered by certain actions, such as: - Chewing - Swallowing - Coughing - Talking - Yawning - Sneezing The pain is usually very sharp and severe but lasts only a short time. If you experience these symptoms, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help determine the cause and discuss possible treatments with you.
Reference Simplification
In glossopharyngeal neuralgia, attacks are brief and occur intermittently, but they cause excruciating pain. Attacks may be triggered by a particular action, such as chewing, swallowing, talking, yawning, coughing, or sneezing.
