Example 225 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 225
Source Text
In mammals, it consists of the bony labyrinth, a hollow cavity in the temporal bone of the skull with a system of passages comprising two main functional parts: The inner ear is found in all vertebrates, with substantial variations in form and function.
Generated Simplification
The inner ear is a part of the body found in all animals with backbones, including humans. It is located inside the skull, within a hollow area of the temporal bone (a bone on the side of your head). The inner ear has two main jobs: - It helps with hearing. - It helps with balance and knowing the position of your body. The structure of the inner ear can vary between different animals, but it always plays an important role in these functions.
Reference Simplification
In mammals, it consists of two main working parts inside a bony labyrinth: The inner ear is found in all vertebrates, with many variations in form and function.

For example, someone with synesthesia might "see" colors when they hear music or "taste" flavors when they read certain words. If you think you might have synesthesia or have questions about it, consider discussing it with a healthcare professional.

Example 231 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 231
Source Text
Myocardial abnormalities occur in about 8 % of patients within weeks of erythema migrans.
Generated Simplification
Some people who develop erythema migrans, a type of rash often associated with Lyme disease, may experience heart-related problems. These issues, called myocardial abnormalities (problems with the heart muscle), happen in about 8% of patients within a few weeks of the rash appearing. If you have erythema migrans and notice symptoms like chest pain, shortness of breath, or an irregular heartbeat, it’s important to talk to a healthcare provider. They can check if your heart is affected and recommend the right treatment if needed.

Up to 8 % of infected people develop heart problems.

Example 232 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 232
Source Text
Oral itraconazole 200 mg orally once a day given until 2 to 4 weeks after all lesions have resolved (typically 3 to 6 months) is the treatment of choice.
Generated Simplification
The recommended treatment is a medication called itraconazole, which is taken by mouth at a dose of 200 mg once a day. You will need to continue taking this medication until all signs of the condition, such as skin lesions, have completely cleared. After that, it is important to keep taking itraconazole for an additional 2 to 4 weeks to ensure the condition does not come back. In most cases, this treatment lasts for about 3 to 6 months in total. Your doctor will guide you on how long you need to stay on the medication based on your progress.
Reference Simplification
Skin infections are treated with itraconazole given by mouth.

Example 235 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 235
Source Text
Among spinal subdural abscesses, methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism involved. Rarely parasites can cause abscesses and this is more common in the developing world.
Generated Simplification
Spinal subdural abscesses are infections that form in the space between the layers of tissue covering the spinal cord. - The most common cause of these infections is a type of bacteria called methicillin-sensitive *Staphylococcus aureus* (often shortened to MSSA). - In rare cases, parasites can also cause these infections. This is more likely to happen in parts of the world where access to healthcare and sanitation may be limited. If you have concerns about spinal infections or symptoms like back pain, fever, or weakness, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help determine the cause and recommend the right treatment.
Reference Simplification
Among spinal subdural abscesses, methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism involved.

Example 236 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 236
Source Text
Periungual warts are more common among patients who bite their nails or who have occupations where their hands are chronically wet such as dishwashers and bartenders. Genital warts manifest as discrete flat to broad-based smooth to velvety papules to rough and pedunculated excrescences on the perineal, perirectal, labial, and penile areas. Perirectal warts often itch.
Generated Simplification
Periungual warts are small growths that appear around the fingernails. They are more likely to develop in people who bite their nails or those who have jobs where their hands are often wet, like dishwashers or bartenders. Genital warts are growths that can appear in areas like the genitals, around the anus, or nearby skin. They can look different from person to person. Some are flat and smooth, while others may be raised, rough, or have a velvety texture. In some cases, they may be on a stalk-like base. Warts around the anus (perirectal warts) often cause itching. If you notice any of these types of warts, it’s important to talk to a healthcare provider. They can help diagnose the condition and discuss treatment options with you.
Reference Simplification
Also called venereal warts or condylomata acuminata, genital warts occur on the penis, anus, vulva, vagina, and cervix. They can be flat, smooth, and velvety or irregular, bumpy growths often with the texture of a small cauliflower. Warts around the anus often itch.

At this stage, the barking cough and noisy breathing (called stridor) may not be as noticeable. Most children who are brought to the emergency room with croup have a mild form of the illness—this happens in about 85% of cases. Severe croup is very uncommon, affecting less than 1% of children.

Example 238 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 238
Source Text
The risk of overdose is increased if flunitrazepam is taken in combination with CNS depressants such as alcohol and opiates.
Generated Simplification
Taking flunitrazepam with other substances that slow down the brain and nervous system, like alcohol or opioid painkillers, can increase the risk of an overdose. If you are using flunitrazepam, it’s important to avoid combining it with these substances. Speak with your doctor if you have any questions or concerns about how flunitrazepam might interact with other medications or substances you use.
Reference Simplification
The overdose will be worse if flunitrazepam is taken with depressants, like alcohol or opiates.

Example 239 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 239
Source Text
The Intersex Society of North America stated that `` For decades, doctors have thought it necessary to treat intersex with a concealment-centered approach, one that features downplaying intersex as much as possible, even to the point of lying to patients about their conditions.
Generated Simplification
For many years, doctors often used a "concealment-centered approach" when treating intersex individuals. This approach focused on minimizing or hiding the fact that a person was intersex. In some cases, this even included withholding information or not fully explaining the condition to the patient. If you have questions about intersex conditions or past medical care, it’s important to talk openly with a healthcare professional you trust. They can provide more information and help address any concerns.
Reference Simplification
About 1 in 100 babies are born with an intersex condition, and 1 in 1000 babies have an intersex condition that the medical community declares needs surgery, in order adhere to binary sex/gender ideals.

Example 240 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 240
Source Text
Biopsy of the lung is usually not indicated unless the pulmonary hypertension is thought to be due to an underlying interstitial lung disease; further, lung biopsies are fraught with risks of bleeding due to the high intrapulmonary blood pressure.
Generated Simplification
A lung biopsy is not usually needed unless doctors suspect that pulmonary hypertension (high blood pressure in the lungs) is caused by an underlying condition called interstitial lung disease. Interstitial lung disease refers to a group of disorders that cause scarring and inflammation in the lungs, which can make it harder to breathe. However, lung biopsies carry certain risks, especially in people with pulmonary hypertension. This is because the high blood pressure in the lungs increases the chance of bleeding during the procedure. If a lung biopsy is being considered, your doctor will carefully weigh the risks and benefits and discuss them with you.
Reference Simplification
Biopsy of the lung is usually not indicated unless the pulmonary hypertension is thought to be due to an underlying interstitial lung disease.

Example 241 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 241
Source Text
Epidemiological studies can only go to prove that an agent could have caused, but not that it did cause, an effect in any particular case: In United States law, epidemiology alone can not prove that a causal association does not exist in general.
Generated Simplification
Studies that look at patterns of health and disease in groups of people (called epidemiological studies) can help identify things that might cause a health problem. However, these studies cannot prove that something definitely caused a specific health issue in one person. In the United States, the law recognizes that these studies alone cannot completely rule out the possibility that something might cause a health problem in general.
Reference Simplification
Epidemiological studies can only go to prove that an agent could have caused disease in population, but not that it did cause, an effect in any particular case.

Example 242 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 242
Source Text
Signs occur along a continuum: no changes to varicose veins (rare) to edema to stasis dermatitis on the lower legs and at the ankles, with or without ulceration (see Table: Clinical Classification of Chronic Venous Insufficiency). Venous stasis dermatitis consists of reddish brown hyperpigmentation, induration, venous ectasia, lipodermatosclerosis (fibrosing subcutaneous panniculitis), and venous stasis ulcers.
Generated Simplification
Signs of this condition can range from no visible changes in the veins (which is uncommon) to more noticeable symptoms. These symptoms may include: - Swelling in the legs (edema). - Skin changes on the lower legs and ankles, known as venous stasis dermatitis. This can happen with or without open sores (ulcers). Venous stasis dermatitis refers to specific skin changes caused by poor blood flow in the veins. These changes may include: - Reddish-brown discoloration of the skin. - Thickened or hardened skin (induration). - Enlarged or twisted veins near the surface of the skin (venous ectasia). - A condition called lipodermatosclerosis, where the fat under the skin becomes inflamed and scarred. - Open sores (venous stasis ulcers) that may take time to heal. If you notice any of these symptoms, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can help determine the cause and recommend treatment options.
Reference Simplification
The discoloration is caused by red blood cells that escape from swollen (distended) veins into the skin. The discolored skin is vulnerable, and even a minor injury, such as that from scratching or a bump, can break it open, resulting in an ulcer.

0.248

0.187

Example 245 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 245
Source Text
A common finding during physical examination is a symmetry or a dominant mass âa mass distinctly different from the surrounding breast tissue. Diffuse fibrotic changes in a quadrant of the breast, usually the upper outer quadrant, are more characteristic of benign disorders; a slightly firmer thickening in one breast but not the other may be a sign of cancer.
Generated Simplification
During a physical exam, doctors may notice changes in the breast that could be important. Here’s what you should know: - Sometimes, a lump or area in the breast feels different from the surrounding tissue. This is called a "dominant mass." - If there are widespread changes in the texture of the breast tissue, especially in the upper outer part of the breast, it is often linked to non-cancerous (benign) conditions. - However, if one breast has a slightly firmer or thicker area that isn’t present in the other breast, it could be a sign of breast cancer. If you notice any unusual changes in your breasts, such as a lump, thickening, or changes in texture, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help determine if further tests are needed.
Reference Simplification
Such a lump may be cancer if it is a firm, distinctive thickening that appears in one breast but not the other. Usually, scattered lumpy changes in the breast, especially the upper outer region, are not cancerous and indicate fibrocystic changes.

Example 246 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 246
Source Text
More advanced breast cancers are characterized by one or more of the following: Fixation of the mass to the chest wall or to overlying skin; Satellite nodules or ulcers in the skin; Exaggeration of the usual skin markings resulting from skin edema caused by invasion of dermal lymphatic vessels (so-called peau d â orange).
Generated Simplification
Advanced breast cancer can show certain signs that may help identify it. These include: - A lump in the breast that feels stuck to the chest wall or the skin above it, meaning it doesn’t move easily. - Small lumps or sores on the skin near the main lump. - Changes in the skin of the breast, such as swelling that makes the skin look dimpled or textured like an orange peel. This happens because the cancer affects tiny lymph vessels in the skin. If you notice any of these changes, it’s important to talk to your doctor as soon as possible. They can help determine the cause and discuss next steps for evaluation or treatment.
Reference Simplification
In more advanced stages, the lump usually adheres to the chest wall or the skin over it. If a breast contains cancer that adheres to the chest wall or skin, this maneuver may make the skin pucker or dimple or make one breast appear different from the other. In very advanced cancer, swollen bumps or festering sores may develop on the skin. Sometimes the skin over the lump is dimpled and leathery and looks like the skin of an orange (peau d â orange) except in color.

Example 248 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 248
Source Text
Myoclonus that occurs when patients are suddenly startled (startle myoclonus) may be an early symptom of Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease.
Generated Simplification
Sudden, jerky muscle movements that happen when someone is startled (called startle myoclonus) can sometimes be an early sign of a rare brain condition known as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD). If you or someone you know is experiencing this symptom, it’s important to talk to a doctor. They can evaluate the situation, check for other possible causes, and decide if further testing is needed.
Reference Simplification
In Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease (a rare degenerative brain disorder), myoclonus becomes more obvious when people are suddenly startled.

Example 250 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 250
Source Text
Vaccinia given after exposure to smallpox, within the first three days, is reported to attenuate the disease considerably, and vaccination up to a week after exposure probably offers some protection from disease or may modify the severity of disease.
Generated Simplification
If someone is exposed to smallpox, getting the smallpox vaccine (called the vaccinia vaccine) within the first three days can significantly reduce how severe the illness becomes. If the vaccine is given later—up to seven days after exposure—it may still provide some protection or make the illness less severe. If you think you’ve been exposed to smallpox, it’s important to contact a healthcare professional right away to discuss vaccination and next steps.
Reference Simplification
This same method using liquid from sores was also used to give people smallpox.

Example 251 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 251
Source Text
Urinary catheterization increases the risk for urinary tract infections.
Generated Simplification
Using a urinary catheter can raise the chance of getting a urinary tract infection (UTI). A urinary catheter is a thin tube placed in the bladder to help drain urine. While it can be necessary for certain medical conditions, it can also make it easier for bacteria to enter the urinary system, which may lead to an infection. If you have a catheter or need one, talk to your healthcare provider about ways to lower the risk of infection. They may recommend steps like keeping the area clean, drinking plenty of fluids, or removing the catheter as soon as it’s no longer needed.
Reference Simplification
Women get urinary tract infections more often than men.

Example 253 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 253
Source Text
Because patients lack the motor responses (eg, withdrawal from painful stimuli) usually used to measure responsiveness, they may be mistakenly thought to be unconscious. Thus, all patients who can not move should have their comprehension tested by requesting eye blinking or vertical eye movements.
Generated Simplification
Sometimes, people who cannot move their body may appear unconscious because they cannot react to pain or other stimuli in the usual ways, like pulling away. However, they might still be fully aware and able to understand what’s happening around them. To check if someone in this situation can understand, doctors or caregivers can ask them to respond in other ways, such as blinking their eyes or moving their eyes up and down. This helps determine if the person is conscious and able to follow instructions, even if they cannot move their body.
Reference Simplification
Because locked-in syndrome can be mistaken for stupor or coma, doctors test people who do not move and appear unresponsive by asking them to open and close their eyes.

Example 254 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 254
Source Text
Bedside neurologic testing Neuropsychologic testing
Generated Simplification
Neurologic Testing at the Bedside and Neuropsychological Testing When doctors perform neurologic testing at the bedside, they are checking how well your brain, nerves, and muscles are working. This type of testing is done during a physical exam and includes simple checks like: - Testing your reflexes (how your body reacts to certain stimuli, like tapping your knee). - Checking your muscle strength and coordination. - Observing how well you can move your eyes, arms, and legs. - Assessing your ability to feel touch, temperature, or pain in different areas of your body. These tests help your doctor quickly identify any problems with your nervous system, which includes your brain, spinal cord, and nerves. Neuropsychological testing is more detailed and focuses on how your brain works in areas like memory, attention, problem-solving, and language. This testing is usually done by a specialist and involves tasks like answering questions, solving puzzles, or remembering information. Why these tests might be needed: - If you’re experiencing symptoms like memory loss, confusion, trouble speaking, or difficulty moving, these tests can help find the cause. - They can also help diagnose conditions like stroke, brain injury, dementia, or other problems affecting the brain and nervous system. What this means for you: - If your doctor recommends these tests, it’s to better understand what might be causing your symptoms. - Bedside neurologic testing is quick and part of a routine exam, while neuropsychological testing may take longer and require a separate appointment. If you have questions about these tests or why they’re being done, talk to your doctor. They can explain what to expect and how the results will guide your care.
Reference Simplification
A doctor 's evaluation Standardized tests of brain function

-0.396
Recall
0.208

-0.111

Example 256 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 256
Source Text
CA 19­-9 and CA 125 are other tumor markers that may be similarly used.
Generated Simplification
CA 19-9 and CA 125 are substances in the blood that doctors sometimes measure to help check for certain types of cancer. These are called "tumor markers." - **CA 19-9**: This marker is often linked to cancers of the pancreas, bile ducts, or stomach. - **CA 125**: This marker is commonly used to monitor ovarian cancer. It’s important to know that these markers are not perfect. High levels of these markers don’t always mean cancer is present, and normal levels don’t always rule it out. If your doctor is testing for these markers, they will explain what the results could mean for your specific situation. Always discuss any questions or concerns with your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
Two other cancer markers, CA 19-9 and CA 125, are similar to CEA and are sometimes elevated in colorectal cancer.

Example 257 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 257
Source Text
Cardiac complications include pericarditis (most commonly) and myocarditis.
Generated Simplification
Heart-related complications can include: - **Pericarditis**: This is when the thin sac around the heart, called the pericardium, becomes inflamed. It is the most common heart issue in this context. - **Myocarditis**: This happens when the heart muscle itself becomes inflamed. If you have concerns about these conditions or symptoms like chest pain, shortness of breath, or an irregular heartbeat, it’s important to talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
People with lupus may have chest pain due to inflammation of the sac around the heart pericarditis). More serious but rare effects on the heart are inflammation of the walls of the coronary arteries (coronary artery vasculitis), which can lead to angina, and inflammation of the heart muscle (myocarditis), which can lead to heart failure.

Example 258 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 258
Source Text
Lifestyle changes have been shown to be effective in reducing (and in the case of diet, reversing) coronary disease: In people with coronary artery disease, aerobic exercise can reduce the risk of mortality.
Generated Simplification
Making changes to your lifestyle can help improve heart health and even reverse some heart problems in certain cases. For example: - **Diet**: Eating a healthy diet can not only improve your heart health but may also help reverse coronary artery disease, which is a condition where the blood vessels supplying your heart become narrowed or blocked. - **Exercise**: Regular aerobic exercise, like walking, jogging, swimming, or cycling, can lower your risk of serious complications and even reduce the chance of dying from heart-related issues. If you have coronary artery disease, these changes can make a big difference. Talk to your doctor about creating a plan that works for you. They can guide you on the best types of exercise and dietary changes for your specific situation.
Reference Simplification
Most people with coronary heart disease can feel healthy for years before they start experiencing symptoms.

Example 261 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 261
Source Text
The pain, lasting seconds to a few minutes, usually begins in the tonsillar region or at the base of the tongue and may radiate to the ipsilateral ear.
Generated Simplification
The pain usually starts in the area around the tonsils (the soft tissue at the back of your throat) or at the base of your tongue. It may last for a few seconds to a few minutes. Sometimes, this pain can spread to the ear on the same side as where it started.
Reference Simplification
The pain usually begins at the back of the tongue, back of the throat, or the area near the tonsils. Sometimes pain spreads to the ear or the area at the back of the jaw. The pain may last several seconds to a few minutes and usually affects only one side of the throat and tongue.

Example 270 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 270
Source Text
Infection ranges from asymptomatic (in 5 to 50 % of cases) to a hemorrhagic fever with a case fatality rate of up to 50 %.
Generated Simplification
The infection can cause a wide range of symptoms, or sometimes no symptoms at all. - In some cases (about 5% to 50%), people may not feel sick or notice any symptoms. This is called being "asymptomatic." - In more severe cases, the infection can lead to a serious illness called hemorrhagic fever. This condition can cause bleeding, fever, and other dangerous symptoms. - The most severe cases of hemorrhagic fever can be life-threatening, with up to 50% of people who develop it not surviving. If you have concerns about symptoms or risks, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional. They can provide guidance based on your specific situation.
Reference Simplification
Some infected people do not have symptoms. Others have mild symptoms, and some have a severe, life -threatening illness. Up to 50 % of people with severe bleeding and fever die.

Example 271 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 271
Source Text
Renovascular hypertension may develop if the abdominal aorta or renal arteries are narrowed.
Generated Simplification
High blood pressure, also called hypertension, can sometimes happen because the blood vessels that supply the kidneys or the main artery in the abdomen (called the abdominal aorta) become too narrow. This condition is known as renovascular hypertension. If you have concerns about high blood pressure or think this might apply to you, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can check for this condition and discuss possible treatments if needed.
Reference Simplification
Kidneys: The kidneys may malfunction because the arteries that supply blood to the kidneys become narrowed. This narrowing can result in high blood pressure.

Example 272 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 272
Source Text
Unlike other dementias, multiple- infarct dementia tends to progress in discrete steps; each episode is accompanied by intellectual decline, sometimes followed by modest recovery.
Generated Simplification
Unlike some other types of dementia, a condition called multiple-infarct dementia usually worsens in noticeable steps rather than gradually. Each step happens after an event, such as a small stroke, which can cause a decline in thinking and memory. Sometimes, there may be a slight improvement after these episodes, but the overall pattern is a step-by-step decline.
Reference Simplification
Unlike dementia caused by Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia may progress in steps. Symptoms may worsen suddenly, then plateau or lessen somewhat. They then become worse months or years later when another stroke occurs.

Source Text
Intractable apneic spells require ventilator support.
Generated Simplification
Some breathing problems, called intractable apneic spells, can be very serious and may not improve on their own. These episodes involve repeated pauses in breathing that do not respond to usual treatments. In such cases, a machine called a ventilator may be needed to help the person breathe. If you or someone you care for experiences severe breathing difficulties, it’s important to seek medical attention immediately. A healthcare professional can determine if ventilator support is necessary.
Reference Simplification
Newborns who have apnea spells that are difficult to treat may need a ventilator (a machine that helps air get in and out of the lungs) to help them breathe.

Example 274 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 274
Source Text
Diagnosis of DiGeorge syndrome is based on clinical findings. An absolute lymphocyte count is done, followed by B- and T- cell counts and evaluation of lymphocyte subsets if leukopenia is detected; blood tests to evaluate T- cell and parathyroid function are done. Ig levels and vaccine titers are measured. If complete DiGeorge syndrome is suspected, the T- cell receptor excision circle (TREC) test should also be done. A lateral chest x-ray may help evaluate thymic shadow. Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) testing can detect the chromosomal deletion in the 22q11 region; standard chromosomal tests to check for other abnormalities may also be done.
Generated Simplification
DiGeorge syndrome is diagnosed by looking at specific signs and running several tests. Here’s how doctors check for it: - **Initial Blood Tests**: Doctors start by measuring the number of lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell) in your blood. If the lymphocyte count is low, they will do more detailed tests to check the levels of B cells and T cells (two important types of immune cells) and look at different types of lymphocytes. - **T-Cell and Parathyroid Function**: Blood tests are also used to see how well your T cells are working and to check the function of your parathyroid glands. The parathyroid glands help control calcium levels in your body. - **Immune System Check**: Doctors measure immunoglobulin (Ig) levels, which are proteins that help fight infections. They may also check how well your body responds to vaccines by measuring vaccine titers (a way to see if your immune system has made antibodies after vaccination). - **Complete DiGeorge Syndrome**: If doctors think you might have a severe form of DiGeorge syndrome, they may do a TREC test. This test looks for specific markers in your blood that show how well your T cells are developing. - **Chest X-Ray**: A chest x-ray might be done to check for the presence of the thymus gland. The thymus is an organ that plays a key role in the immune system, and its absence or abnormal size can be a clue for DiGeorge syndrome. - **Genetic Testing**: A test called FISH (fluorescent in situ hybridization) can identify a missing piece of chromosome 22 in the 22q11 region, which is a common cause of DiGeorge syndrome. Sometimes, additional genetic tests are done to look for other possible chromosomal problems. If you or your child are being evaluated for DiGeorge syndrome, your doctor will guide you through these tests and explain the results. Be sure to discuss any questions or concerns with your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
Sometimes imaging tests (such as chest x-rays and echocardiography). Doctors suspect DiGeorge syndrome based on symptoms. Blood tests are done for the following reasons: To determine the total number of blood cells and the number of T and B cells; To evaluate how well T cells and the parathyroid gland are functioning; To determine how well the body produces immunoglobulins in response to vaccines. A chest x-ray may be taken to check the size of the thymus gland.
BERTScore
Precision
0.012
Recall
0.221
F1
0.116

Example 275 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 275
Source Text
It had a cranial capacity of 400-500 cc. In 2006, Lee Berger announced the Taung Child probably was killed by an eagle or other large predatory bird since the similarity of damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child is of that seen in modern primates that are known to have been killed by eagles.
Generated Simplification
The Taung Child was a young early human ancestor whose skull was discovered in South Africa. Scientists estimate that its brain size was about 400-500 cubic centimeters, which is much smaller than the average human brain today. In 2006, a researcher named Lee Berger suggested that the Taung Child was likely killed by a large bird, such as an eagle. This idea is based on the damage found on the skull and eye sockets, which looks similar to injuries seen in modern primates that have been attacked by eagles.
Reference Simplification
This conclusion was reached by noting similarities in the damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child to the skulls of primates known to have been killed by modern eagles. The left hand side of the endocast of the Taung child is covered in beautiful calcium carbonate crystals.

Example 278 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 278
Source Text
Vasoconstriction can change a lesion that does not limit blood flow into a severe or complete stenosis. Symptoms of unstable angina or myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, or rest pain in the limbs may develop when unstable plaques rupture and acutely occlude a major artery, with superimposition of thrombosis or embolism.
Generated Simplification
When blood vessels tighten (a process called vasoconstriction), it can make a partially blocked area in the blood vessel worse, sometimes leading to a severe or completely blocked blood flow. This can cause serious problems, such as: - Chest pain or discomfort (unstable angina) or a heart attack if the heart's blood supply is affected. - A stroke if the blood flow to the brain is suddenly blocked. - Pain in the arms or legs, even when resting, if blood flow to these areas is severely reduced. These issues can happen when unstable plaques (fatty deposits in the blood vessels) break open. This can lead to a sudden blockage in a major artery due to the formation of a blood clot (thrombosis) or a piece of the clot traveling to another part of the body (embolism). If you experience any of these symptoms, it’s important to seek medical attention immediately. Discuss any concerns or risks with your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
The first symptom of a narrowed artery may be pain or cramps at times when blood flow can not keep up with the tissues â need for oxygen.

Precision
0.041
Recall
0.236
F1
0.138

Example 279 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 279
Source Text
According to the fifth Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), DID includes `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' that alternate control of the individual 's behavior, accompanied by the inability to recall personal information beyond what is expected through normal forgetfulness.
Generated Simplification
Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) is a mental health condition. It involves having two or more distinct identities or personality states. These identities take turns controlling a person’s behavior. People with DID may also have trouble remembering personal information. This memory loss goes beyond normal forgetfulness, meaning it’s more severe than simply forgetting small details. If you think you or someone you know might have symptoms of DID, it’s important to talk to a healthcare professional for an evaluation and guidance.
Reference Simplification
According to the DSM-IV, the symptoms of DID are `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' (which are often called `` alters ''), as well as forgetting things that people normally do n't forget.

Example 290 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 290
Source Text
Infants with hypothermia may feel cold when touched, with bright red skin and unusual lack of energy.
Generated Simplification
If a baby has hypothermia, their body temperature is too low. You might notice the following signs: - Their skin feels cold when you touch them. - Their skin may look bright red. - They might seem unusually tired or have very little energy. If you notice these symptoms, it’s important to contact a healthcare professional right away. Hypothermia can be serious and needs prompt attention.

Source Text
Hepatitis E causes different histological patterns that depend on the host 's background.
Generated Simplification
Hepatitis E is a type of liver infection caused by the hepatitis E virus. The way this infection affects the liver can vary depending on the person’s overall health and medical history. For example, factors like pre-existing liver conditions or a weakened immune system may influence how the liver tissue is affected. If you have concerns about hepatitis E or your liver health, it’s important to talk to your doctor. They can help determine if you are at risk and discuss any necessary tests or treatments.

Instead, hepatitis B is spread by blood or body fluids from an infected person.

Example 297 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 297
Source Text
In hypoxic pulmonary hypertension (WHO Group III), the low levels of oxygen are thought to cause vasoconstriction or tightening of pulmonary arteries.
Generated Simplification
In a condition called hypoxic pulmonary hypertension (classified as WHO Group III), low oxygen levels in the blood can make the blood vessels in the lungs tighten. These blood vessels, called pulmonary arteries, carry blood from the heart to the lungs. When they tighten, it becomes harder for blood to flow through them, which can increase pressure in the lungs.
Reference Simplification
Treatment for hypoxic and miscellaneous varieties of pulmonary hypertension have not been established.

Precision
0.339

0.49

0.414

Example 298 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 298
Source Text
Arterial blood carries oxygen from inhaled air to all of the cells of the body, and venous blood carries carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism by cells, to the lungs to be exhaled.
Generated Simplification
The blood in your arteries carries oxygen from the air you breathe to all the cells in your body. This oxygen is essential for your cells to work properly. The blood in your veins does the opposite job. It carries carbon dioxide, which is a waste product made when your cells use oxygen, back to your lungs. Your lungs then remove this carbon dioxide when you exhale (breathe out).
Reference Simplification
Red blood cells carry oxygen and carbon dioxide around our body.

0.547

0.452

Example 299 | Profile: layperson | Language: English | ID/Index: 299
Source Text
The kinetoplastids (also known as ` kinetoplastea ') are a group of single-cell flagellate protozoa, including a number of parasites responsible for serious diseases in humans and other animals, as well as various forms found in soil and aquatic environments.
Generated Simplification
Kinetoplastids are tiny, single-celled organisms that move using a whip-like structure called a flagellum. Some of these organisms can cause serious diseases in humans and animals. While many kinetoplastids are harmless and live in soil or water, certain types act as parasites, meaning they live in or on a host and can harm it.
Reference Simplification
